question.text: Why is STT (Securities Transaction Tax) NOT applicable on interest rate derivatives?

answer.text: STT is levied only on equity and equity derivative transactions as per the Finance Act; debt and interest rate derivatives are exempt
STT was abolished for all derivatives in 2018
STT applies to IRD but at a zero rate
STT is applicable but collected by RBI instead of the exchange

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The Finance Act specifies STT only on transactions in equity shares, equity derivatives, and units of equity-oriented mutual funds. Interest rate derivatives, currency derivatives, and commodity derivatives are not subject to STT. **The Trap:** STT was not abolished - it still applies to equity derivatives. There is no 'zero rate' provision. RBI doesn't collect STT. **Cross-Exam Context:** STT applicability is a frequently tested topic. In Series VIII (equity), STT applies. In Series I (currency) and Series IV (IRD), it does not. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** No STT on IRD is a cost advantage over equity derivatives. This makes IRD trading relatively cheaper for high-frequency strategies.
